segments.text: So in my slides here, I'm going to talk about debugging and understanding NLP models.
And this is how to tell when, for example, both your implementations are wrong.
And for example, your underlying assumptions are wrong or your model is failing on particular segments and data or stuff like that.
So I'm going to go through a variety of things that can go wrong with your experiments basically.
So a typical situation is you've implemented some NLP system, you know, based on neural networks, of course, because that's what we use nowadays.
And you've looked at the code, it basically looks okay.
But it has low accuracy or it makes incomprehensible errors.
And you would like to fix these or you'd like to improve the accuracy or something like that. So what do I do?
And I think there's three dimensions of how you can understand your model and your model behavior.
The first one is debugging the implementation. So it's identifying problems that you have when you implemented something.
A second thing is actionable evaluation. So identifying typical error cases and how you can do to fix them.
And finally, interpreting predictions or interpreting what's happening inside the model.
And this can maybe give you a deeper idea about what's happening in happening in individual cases.
And there's a lot of reasons why you might want to do that.
Both like to make your models better and also, for example, if you want to be sure that your prediction isn't doing something illegal, like discriminating against people.
Due to protected attributes or other things like that.
So there's a number of reasons why you'd want to do that.
So the first two and that Nishant is mainly going to talk about the second one. So going right into it.
So in neural network models, debugging is really important because they're opaque.
They're unpredictable. And if you make little mistakes, they can cause big problems with your output.
And another thing is that everything is a hyperparameter, including your network size, your model variations, your batch size, your strategy, your optimizer, and your learning rate.

And finally, unlike kind of more traditional machine learning methods, like logistic progression or support vector machines or something like that, you might have studied in your machine learning class.

Stochastic optimization has no guarantees about convergence. Your loss might go down, then it might go up and there might be absolutely nothing wrong with your training or it might be, you know, a serious problem. So that's another issue to deal with.
So first I'd like to go into possible causes of problems with your implementation.
And I'm going to break them down into a typology and based on what part of the typology you're running into problems with, you will need to fix them in different ways.
So your first goal when you're experiencing the problem is identifying why you're experiencing the problem because that will lead you to a solution.

So for training time problems, there's a bunch of things that could be wrong. The first is a lack of model capacity. So your model is not able to model the phenomena that you want to model in the first place.
You could have a poor training algorithm.
You could just have a bug in your code at training time.
Another thing is test time problems. And these can include a disconnect between what you're doing at training time and what you're testing at testing time.
Failure of search algorithms.
And another thing you'll want to deal with is overfitting. So you're actually doing well on the training set, but you're doing poorly on the test set.
Finally, you could have an optimized mismatch between the function you're optimizing at evaluation time and what you're actually evaluating.
Sorry, the function that you're optimizing a training time in what you're actually evaluating at test time.

And my best piece of advice for figuring out why things are going wrong is don't try to do all of them at once and rather start from the top and work it down because the ones at the top are often easier to diagnose.
So looking at how you can debug systems at training time, there's a number of ways you can do this.
The most important thing for training time issues is looking at the loss function calculated on the training set.
And what I mean by this is don't look, we talked about how we can't optimize error or accuracy easily. So instead we optimize likelihood.

So you might want to look at accuracy to see whether your model is working well, but I would urge you first to look at your likelihood or your loss function on the training set instead of your accuracy on the test set, for example, to diagnose these very problems.
And the sorts of things you want to look at are, is the loss function going down. So is it, you know, converging into a good place.

So in general, if this is your, your loss. The first thing you should know is like what is a good loss. In most cases, a good loss is zero, like log likelihood, the best loss you can achieve is zero. So you have zero down here.
And this is essentially a good loss function.
Something like that, especially if this is a relatively high number is usually a bad loss function.

Something like that on your training set is a very bad loss function, something, something is going seriously wrong. If you see this on your dev set that could be, or your test set that could be overfitting.
But if you're seeing that on your training set, that's usually symptomatic of a problem. So these are things that you should be knowing.
And is it going down basically to zero if you run training long enough for many epochs over your training data. So if it's not going down to zero and it's sticking up here, then that's also an issue.
And if it's not going down to close to zero on whatever training set you're training on, let's see, you make your training set extremely small.
At least in that case, it should go down to zero. Otherwise, you might have a serious problem in your implementation.
So these are good things to check first when you're training a model.
And there's a number of reasons why this might not be helping or why this might not be happening. So your model might be too weak.
And so in general, larger models tend to perform better, especially if you're using a pre-trained model. And this is just an example from the key five paper where they scale up the T five model.
From relatively small model to what at the time was a very large model of 11 billion parameters. Now this is, you know, moderately sized model or maybe even small model by some standards.
But anyway, you can see that it continues to increase.
One really interesting phenomenon is that actually larger models can learn faster or at least with fewer steps than smaller models.
And so this is an interesting example. This paper on neural scaling was, it's a very influential paper. But basically what they show is the darker purple ones are smaller models.

The yellow ones are bigger models. And what you can see here is the purple model and on the left side, they have the number of tokens processed on the right side, they have the number of compute for the amount of compute.

And so what you can see is if you just look at the number of tokens processed, the larger the model, the faster it converges, which is maybe a little bit surprising, maybe a little bit, or maybe like some people have the intuition that this should be the case.
But when I first saw this, I found it a little bit surprising because I thought it would be so large and noisy that the model would have trouble fitting, you know, fitting the data as quickly.
But there's actually a good reason for this. Does anyone have a guess about why this is the case?
We've talked a little bit about the underlying phenomenon for this in previous classes.
So I think that's the only thing that I can do is to think back to some of the things you see.
Yeah, so just to repeat, there's a lot of different parameters so it can kind of converge along a lot of different dimensions.
So if we think back to the like model pruning class and other stuff like that, part of the reason why we conclude large models. So if,
you know, it's not that efficient, it's because only like a small number of the parameters are actually useful.
And so if you start out with a much larger model, it's more likely to have useful subsets of the parameters basically, which is called the lottery ticket hypothesis.
There's a famous favorite full of the lottery ticket hypothesis that examines this phenomenon.

One one interesting thing is you can see that even if you scale up the compute, even if you measure based on compute, the larger models eventually surpass the smaller models in terms of how efficient they are at modeling the data.
And that's just because models tend to learn well for a while and then they basically reach their capacity and stop learning well or they start learning very slowly.
And once you get to that point, the larger models work better. So there's a kind of counter intuitive thing that if you want to train faster, you have.
